_meta.notes: Chapter 0: Introduction / Abakada. Teach-only orientation. HelloChinese-style centered layout with large emoji visuals.

cards.body:
Tagalog uses the Latin alphabet.
No new script to learn!
The traditional Filipino alphabet has 20 letters.
cards.callout:
A  Ba  Ka  Da
E  Ga  Ha  I
La  Ma  Na  Ng
O  Pa  Ra  Sa
Ta  U  Wa  Ya

cards.highlight: What you see is what you say
cards.body:
Tagalog is mostly phonetic.

Unlike English where 'tough', 'through', and 'though' all sound different, Tagalog letters have consistent sounds.

Three things to know:

• The rolled R
• 'Ng' is its own letter (like in 'singing')
• The glottal stop (a quick pause, like 'uh-oh')

These Tagalog words made it into English:

• *Karaoke*
• *Boondocks* (from bundok - mountain)
• *Sari-sari* (variety store)

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: American soldiers in the Philippines borrowed 'bundok' (mountain) - it became 'boondocks' meaning a remote area.

cards.body:
Tagalog has no he/she distinction.

*Siya* means he, she, or they.

One word for everyone.

cards.highlight: You're about to learn your first Tagalog words. Let's go!

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: No quizzes in this lesson. Teach-only orientation.

alam_mo_ba: The Philippines has over 180 languages, but Tagalog (Filipino) is the national lingua franca.
The word 'karaoke' comes from Japanese, but the karaoke machine was invented by a Filipino named Roberto del Rosario.
